result.coverage_notes:
Threat model: SQL injection via raw SQL. I traced every dangerous SQL sink in scope and found no defensible injection path; all DB access uses the Django ORM with proper parameterization.

Files read in full / closely traced:
- weblate/utils/db.py: The only raw cursor.execute calls (adjust_similarity_threshold) are either a static string ("SELECT show_limit()") or parameterized ("SELECT set_limit(%s)", [value]) with a computed float, never user-controlled string interpolation. The custom PostgreSQL lookups (PostgreSQLSearchLookup, PostgreSQLSubstringLookup, PostgreSQLRegexLookup) subclass Django's Lookup/PatternLookup/Regex. Their get_rhs_op overrides return strings like f"%% {rhs} = true" / f"ILIKE {rhs}", but `rhs` here is Django's compiled rhs SQL placeholder ("%s"), not the raw value — the value flows through process_rhs as a bound parameter. process_lhs only concatenates compiler-produced lhs SQL with "|| ''" or wraps it in UPPER(). No user value reaches SQL text. PG_TRGM/PG_DROP .format() strings are defined but unused in the application path (indexes are created via postgres_indexes in migrations).
- weblate/utils/search.py: The full search-query parser. All field/term handling builds Django Q() objects with kwargs lookups; values flow as ORM parameters. Field names are resolved through fixed allow-list maps (PLAIN_FIELDS, NONTEXT_FIELDS, STRING_FIELD_MAP, EXACT_FIELD_MAP, CHANGE_FIELD_MAP) and unknown fields raise SearchQueryError. Regex search uses __trgm_regex / __iregex which Django parameterizes; the glossary has:glossary path builds an iregex template but escapes terms via re_escape and the regex string is still a bound parameter. No string-formatted SQL.
- weblate/memory/models.py: MemoryQuerySet.filter rewrites source/target/origin to __md5=MD5(Value(...)) and lookup() uses source__trgm_search=text plus FK filters — all ORM, parameterized. adjust_similarity_threshold is called only with computed float thresholds.
- weblate/lang/models.py: no raw SQL, no cursor, no .extra/.raw.
- Ordering params (attacker-influenced sort_by): weblate/trans/models/unit.py order_by_request strictly allow-lists sort tokens against available_sort_choices/countable_sort_choices before order_by(*sort_list); weblate/screenshots/views.py uses sort_ordering[sort_by] dict lookup (KeyError on unknown). No raw field injection.

Repo-wide greps for cursor.execute, .raw(, RawSQL, QuerySet.extra(, f"SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE, and %-formatted SQL returned only: test files, the parameterized utils/db.py calls, a static "SHOW server_version" in utils/requirements.py, and VCS subprocess .execute() calls (list-form git/hg commands, out of this SQL threat model's scope). gettext("...%s") usages are translation formatting on already-validated data, not SQL.

Could not fully rule out: dynamic behavior of third-party pg_trgm lookups at the psycopg layer was reasoned about from Django's Lookup contract rather than executed; and I did not exhaustively read every migration file (migrations are not attacker-reachable at runtime). Nothing observed suggests a reachable SQLi.

Hunted SQL injection across the named in-scope surfaces and followed call chains.

Files read in full or in relevant part:
- weblate/utils/db.py: the only cursor.execute calls are adjust_similarity_threshold ("SELECT show_limit()", "SELECT set_limit(%s)" with [value] params) and requirements.py "SHOW server_version" — both static SQL or parameterized. The custom lookups (PostgreSQLSearchLookup/SubstringLookup get_rhs_op return f"ILIKE {rhs}" / f"%% {rhs} = true") use rhs = the SQL placeholder produced by process_rhs (param_pattern "%s"), not the user value; the value flows through Django's params list. PG_TRGM/PG_DROP .format() index-DDL constants are defined but I found no caller in the repo (grep across weblate/*.py shows only the definition), so they are not a reachable sink.
- weblate/utils/search.py (full): the search query language (the "q" param, also reused by addons/autotranslate.py) is parsed with pyparsing into Django ORM Q() objects. Field names are resolved through allow-list maps (PLAIN_FIELDS/NONTEXT_FIELDS/STRING_FIELD_MAP/EXACT_FIELD_MAP) with SearchQueryError on unknown fields; match values become ORM lookup values (parameterized). Regex/iregex (trgm_regex, glossary has_field template.format with re_escape) pass user input as a query *value*, not concatenated SQL.
- weblate/trans/models/unit.py order(): sort fields validated against available_sort_choices / countable_sort_choices allow-lists before order_by.
- weblate/screenshots/views.py order_by(*self.sort_ordering[sort_by]): dict lookup of fixed orderings.
- lang/models.py and memory/models.py: order_by uses static column names; no .extra/.raw/RawSQL/string-formatted SQL. memory/models.py:695 and others use str.format on non-SQL text.

Repo-wide greps for cursor.execute, .raw(, RawSQL, .extra(, and f-string SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE returned only the above (plus test files). No attacker-influenced fragment reaches a raw SQL string. No defensible SQLi finding; reporting zero is the honest result.

Scope reviewed in full: weblate/machinery/ (base.py, forms.py, views.py, openai.py, llm.py header, and provider URL fields across deepl/modernmt/libretranslate/microsoft/sap/ollama/azure via forms.py), plus weblate/accounts/avatar.py, weblate/utils/requests.py, weblate/utils/outbound.py, and weblate/utils/validators.py.

THREAT-MODEL CONCLUSION: This SSRF cluster has already been comprehensively hardened, defeating the requested attack classes (metadata 169.254.169.254, internal services, blind SSRF, DNS rebinding):

1. Trust boundary on outbound calls (base.py:313-352 request()). allow_private_targets = "_project" not in self.settings (base.py:209). Project-scoped configs (forms.py:85 injects _project; views.py:339 EditMachineryProjectView.allow_private_targets=False) go through fetch_validated_url with allow_private_targets=False and ALLOWED_MACHINERY_DOMAINS allowlist. Only site-wide configs (gated by the machinery.edit superadmin permission, views.py:331-334) use the unvalidated fetch_url — an intentional, documented admin trust boundary, not an attacker-reachable SSRF.

2. Project-config creation requires project.edit (views.py:360-363) and validates URL/hostname at form time via validate_machinery_url / validate_machinery_hostname (forms.py:97-109), which reject private/non-public IPs (validators.py:793-809 -> outbound.py validate_untrusted_hostname).

3. Runtime defenses in utils/requests.py are robust against DNS rebinding and redirect-based bypass: _request_with_redirects re-validates EACH redirect hop (validate_request_url, line 224) and validates the actual connected PEER IP per response (PeerIPHTTPAdapter + _validate_response_peer, lines 291-314), closing the resolve-time-vs-connect-time TOCTOU.

4. outbound.py is mature: it unwraps IPv4-mapped IPv6, IPv4-compat ::N.N.N.N, NAT64 64:ff9b::/96, 6to4 2002::/16, and rejects special-use prefixes before consulting is_global — blocking the common ipaddress.is_global rebinding tricks.

5. URL schemes are constrained to http/https (validators.py:518-524 WeblateURLValidator.schemes), preventing file://gopher:// scheme bypass.

6. avatar.py download_avatar_image uses raw fetch_url, but the URL is built from server-side AVATAR_URL_PREFIX with only an MD5 hash of the email embedded (avatar.py:33-37) — no attacker-controlled host/path reaches the request, so no SSRF.

Could not exhaustively dynamic-test ALLOWED_MACHINERY_DOMAINS default contents or every provider's runtime URL composition under custom settings, but static review shows all machinery outbound requests funnel through base.py request() with the validated path for non-superadmin configs. No defensible attacker-controlled source-to-sink SSRF path was found; reporting zero findings rather than padding with the by-design superadmin boundary.

Audited the full weblate/gitexport module (views.py, utils.py, models.py, tasks.py, apps.py) plus the call chain into URL routing (weblate/urls.py routes projects/git -> git_export), the URL converters (weblate/utils/urls.py GitPathConverter/ObjectPathConverter), object resolution (weblate/utils/views.py parse_path), access control (weblate/auth/models.py check_access/check_access_component/can_access_component), and error sanitization (weblate/trans/util.py cleanup_repo_url/sanitize_backend_error_message).

Threat-model classes were each traced to ground and found mitigated:

1) OS command injection -> NOT present. The sole external-process sink is GitHTTPBackendWrapper.__init__ (views.py:385) subprocess.Popen([git_http_backend], env=..., ...). It is list-form with no shell=True; the binary path comes from find_git_http_backend() (utils.py, server-controlled via `git --exec-path` / fixed GIT_PATHS). All attacker influence flows into CGI env vars (REQUEST_METHOD, QUERY_STRING, CONTENT_TYPE, PATH_TRANSLATED) and the request body on stdin -- the standard, intended git-http-backend CGI contract, not a shell. The secondary execute(["cat-file","--batch-check"], stdin=...) in get_precheck_failure_reason (views.py:230) is also list-form; the stdin revisions are constrained by WANT_REVISION_LINE_RE to [0-9a-f]{40,64} and capped (MAX_PRECHECK_PKT_LINES=32, MAX_PRECHECK_REVISIONS=32).

2) Path traversal -> NOT reachable. PATH_TRANSLATED = os.path.join(obj.full_path, git_request) (views.py:369). obj.full_path is derived from a DB-resolved Component (parse_path uses get_object_or_404 on project/component/category slugs -- no filesystem path from raw input). git_request is bound by GitPathConverter regex `(info/|git-upload-pack|git-receive-pack)[a-z0-9_/-]*` (utils/urls.py:18), which forbids '.', so no `..` segments; the additional lstrip("/\\") only strips leading separators. ObjectPathConverter segments are `[^/]+` and resolved via DB lookups, not concatenated into a filesystem path.

3) Broken access control / private repo exposure -> NOT present. Access is enforced twice: parse_path calls request.user.check_access(project) and check_access_component(component) (raising Http404 on denial), then git_export requires request.user.has_perm("vcs.access", obj) (views.py:313) before any backend invocation. Anonymous users on private projects get 404/401. The is_repo_link branch redirects via reverse() to the linked component, which re-enters git_export and re-runs the same checks.

4) Credential leakage -> mitigated. Upstream URLs are only surfaced when can_view (vcs.view) is true (get_upstream_location, views.py:87), and even then via cleanup_repo_url which strips user:pass@. Backend stderr returned to clients is run through sanitize_backend_error_message (repo URLs, full_path, and host/URL regexes redacted) and the missing-revision/push paths use canned messages. Dedicated tests (tests.py: test_reject_push_no_url_leak_without_vcs_view, test_missing_revision_error_message_without_vcs_view, full_path not in message) confirm this.

Auth helper (authenticate, views.py:255) validates Basic creds via User.objects.get(username=..., auth_token__key=code) and is_active; tokens are high-entropy so brute force is impractical and there is no injectable surface. No SQL string-building, deserialization, or template rendering in scope.

Conclusion: no defensible finding. The module appears to have been specifically hardened (regex-restricted converter, double authz, error sanitization, bounded precheck) against exactly this threat model. Reporting zero findings rather than padding with theoretical nits.

findings.title: Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
findings.source: GitMergeRequestBase.configure_fork_remote() builds push_url via authenticate_url(http_url, credentials), embedding the configured username and API token as 'username:token@host'. Token originates from the *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']).
findings.sink: self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO) -> weblate.vcs logger -> log aggregation / Sentry breadcrumbs
findings.data_flow: get_credentials() reads token from settings -> push() -> fork()/configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) -> push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials) (URL now contains plaintext token, git.py:1493-1502) -> self.log(... push_url, level=INFO) at git.py:1422. Emitted whenever a merge-request backend configures its fork remote during a push.
findings.exploitability: Requires read access to Weblate VCS logs/Sentry breadcrumbs. Impact is leakage of an org-scoped Git provider token to anyone with log access.
findings.preconditions: A GitHub/GitLab/Gitea merge-request VCS backend is configured with credentials and a push is performed.

findings.title: Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args
findings.source: component.repo / component.push set by project admins may contain embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:token@host/repo.git). For clone the URL is placed directly into the subprocess argv via GitRepository._clone (git.py:341-355).
findings.sink: Repository._popen builds text_cmd = ' '.join(args) and passes it to cls.add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...) at base.py:519-525, forwarding to Sentry/error breadcrumbs unsanitized.
findings.data_flow: admin sets component.repo with embedded password -> clone()/clone_from()/_clone() -> _popen(['git','clone','--', source, target]) -> text_cmd = ' '.join(args) (base.py:476) includes the full URL with credentials -> add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...). sanitize_error_message() is only applied to process output on failure (base.py:527), never to text_cmd, so the credential-bearing command string is recorded verbatim.
findings.exploitability: Needs access to breadcrumb/error-tracking data. Risk is leakage of repo credentials to a broader set of operators/Sentry viewers than intended.
findings.preconditions: A component repo/push URL embeds credentials in the URL and a git operation runs.

result.coverage_notes:
Reviewed the file-upload / path-construction / archive-extraction cluster end to end and found no defensible vulnerability. Files read in full or in relevant part: weblate/trans/views/files.py (upload/download handlers), weblate/utils/files.py (is_excluded, is_path_within_resolved_directory, read_file_bytes, size limits), weblate/utils/zip.py (validate_zip_member_path, validate_zip_member_type, get_safe_zip_member_target, iter_safe_zip_members, ZipSafetyLimits zip-bomb guards), weblate/utils/validators.py (validate_filename rejecting ../, absolute, and is_excluded folders), weblate/trans/models/translation.py (handle_upload, handle_replace, handle_add_upload, load_uploaded_file), weblate/trans/models/component.py (get_validated_component_filename, add_new_language, get_language_filename path build), weblate/addons/cdn.py (CDN file writes), weblate/screenshots/views.py + models.py (image upload), weblate/formats/convert.py (IDML ZipFile), weblate/trans/backups.py (project backup restore zip member validation), weblate/vcs/base.py + git.py (symlink resolution and zip exclusion).

Key reasons no finding was reported:
1. Translation upload writes to self.get_filename() = os.path.join(component.full_path, self.filename), a fixed server-side path; the uploaded file's name (fileobj.name) only flows into the format parser, never into the write target. No traversal sink reached by attacker-controlled filename.
2. All ZIP extraction (backups, IDML, git zip import) goes through validate_zip_member_path / get_safe_zip_member_target / iter_safe_zip_members which reject .., absolute paths, drive letters, null bytes, symlinks, special files, and re-verify the resolved destination is inside the target via is_path_within_resolved_directory. Zip-slip and zip-bomb are both mitigated.
3. add_new_language derives the language code from a real Language object selected via fuzzy_get_strict and applies language_regex + check_file_is_valid before writing; the filemask substitution is not arbitrary attacker input.
4. CDN addon writes use fixed UUID-based paths and require addon-install (admin) privilege; output filenames are derived from validated translation paths via relative_to and validate_output_filename.
5. Screenshot uploads use Django's ScreenshotField storage with sanitized names; no os.path.join with raw client filename.

Could not fully rule out: deep behavior of third-party translate-toolkit/translation_finder parsers invoked during format loading (out of repo scope), and runtime settings overrides (e.g. LOCALIZE_CDN_PATH) that an operator could misconfigure. No anonymous/low-priv remote arbitrary write/read path was demonstrable from the in-scope code.

findings.title: Authenticated daemon client can extract all private keys (mnemonics/entropy) over the WebSocket keychain RPC
findings.source: WebSocket message command="get_all_private_keys" handled in WebSocketServer.handle_message -> keychain_commands
findings.sink: Keychain.get_all_private_keys() returning sk + entropy, serialized as hex in the RPC response (get_all_private_keys, lines 317-326)
findings.data_flow: server.py:incoming_connection -> handle_message (command in keychain_commands) -> KeychainServer.handle_command("get_all_private_keys") -> get_all_private_keys() -> get_keychain_for_request(request).get_all_private_keys() -> response contains pk + entropy.hex(). The only gates are (a) mutual TLS (ssl_context_for_server sets verify_mode=CERT_REQUIRED with the node's private CA in chia/server/server.py:71) and (b) keyring unlocked. Any client presenting a cert signed by the local private CA therefore obtains full master secret material.
findings.preconditions: Attacker must hold/read the private-CA-signed client cert+key on the local filesystem, and the keyring must be unlocked. Local access, not remote/anonymous.
findings.exploitability: Local only. Daemon binds to localhost (self_hostname default localhost) and enforces CERT_REQUIRED, so a remote attacker or browser page without the client cert cannot connect at the TLS layer. The keychain RPC has no per-client authorization beyond possession of the shared node cert, so any local process that can read the SSL material can exfiltrate every private key. Largely by design but the highest-impact reachable behavior in scope.

findings.title: Client-controlled kc_user/kc_service select arbitrary keychain partition (no per-client isolation)
findings.source: WebSocket message data fields request["kc_user"] and request["kc_service"], fully attacker-controllable in a raw message
findings.sink: Keychain(user=user, service=service) construction in get_keychain_for_request, used by all key read/write/delete operations
findings.data_flow: handle_message -> KeychainServer.handle_command -> e.g. get_all_private_keys/delete_all_keys -> get_keychain_for_request(request): user=request.get('kc_user'), service=request.get('kc_service') -> new Keychain(user,service). Because the partition is taken verbatim from the request, any authenticated client can target the default keychain or any other partition; partitioning provides no security boundary between clients sharing the node cert. delete_all_keys with an attacker-chosen partition is a destructive primitive.
findings.preconditions: Same as above: valid private-CA client cert and unlocked keyring.
findings.exploitability: Local, authenticated. Not a privilege-boundary crossing in practice since all clients share one trust anchor, but keychain partitioning is not an authorization control.
findings.code_excerpt:
user = request.get('kc_user', self._default_keychain.user)
service = request.get('kc_service', self._default_keychain.service)
...
keychain = Keychain(user=user, service=service)

findings.title: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
findings.source: Network position: an active man-in-the-middle on the path between the SDK consumer process and advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (malicious Wi-Fi/router, ARP/DNS spoof, compromised upstream proxy).
findings.sink: websockets.connect(..., ssl=ssl.SSLContext() ...) at websocket_base.py:150 establishing the WSS connection.
findings.data_flow: WSBase.open() -> _run_coroutine_threadsafe(open_async()) -> open_async() (websocket_base.py:129-151) calls websockets.connect with ssl=ssl.SSLContext() whenever base_url starts with 'wss://'. A bare ssl.SSLContext() constructor leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so the server certificate and hostname are NOT validated. The authenticated JWT bearer token produced by _build_subscription_message() at websocket_base.py:561 via jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(self.api_key, self.api_secret) is then transmitted over this unverified channel during subscribe_async() (await self.websocket.send at line 267). An active MITM can present any self-signed certificate, complete the handshake, capture the JWT, and observe/inject account and market stream data.
findings.preconditions: Attacker has active on-path/MITM capability. The default base_url is wss://, so the default configuration is affected. JWT is short-lived (120s), which bounds replay value but does not prevent live interception or tampering.
findings.exploitability: Requires an active on-path attacker; no special privilege within the SDK. Fix: use ssl.create_default_context() (CERT_REQUIRED + hostname check) or omit the ssl argument so websockets applies its secure default.

result.reason: Confirmed by reading the code. configure_fork_remote() at git.py:1413-1424 builds push_url via authenticate_url() (git.py:1493-1502), which embeds username:token@host using only urllib.parse.quote (URL-encoding, not redaction). The resulting URL still contains the plaintext API token. It is passed to self.log(f"Configuring fork remote '{remote_name}': {push_url}", level=logging.INFO). The base log() method (base.py:351-352) does NOT sanitize: it calls LOGGER.log(level, "%s: %s", cls._cmd, message) verbatim. sanitize_error_message (base.py:547-548) is a no-op and is only applied to error paths (base.py:502,527), never to log(). So the token reaches the weblate.vcs logger at INFO whenever a merge-request backend (GitHub/GitLab/Gitea/Pagure/Bitbucket) configures its fork remote during push, and the http push_scheme branch is taken. The token comes from *_CREDENTIALS settings (e.g. GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host]['token']). This is a legitimate CWE-532 credential-exposure / logging-hygiene defect. It is not attacker-controlled and not an injection; impact requires read access to logs / Sentry breadcrumbs where the secret is then exposed, so Low severity is accurate.
result.exploit_sketch: Configure a component to push via a merge-request backend (e.g. GitHub) with GITHUB_CREDENTIALS[host] = {username, token}, where the push_scheme resolves to http(s) rather than ssh. Trigger a push (Weblate auto-push or admin "Push" action). push() -> fork() -> configure_fork_remote(ssh_url, http_url, credentials) executes the else-branch (push_url = authenticate_url(http_url, credentials)), producing https://username:TOKEN@host/.... self.log(..., level=logging.INFO) emits this line to the weblate.vcs logger. Anyone with read access to application logs or Sentry breadcrumbs (ops staff, log-aggregation users, or an attacker who has compromised log storage) recovers the plaintext API token and can act as that user against the upstream Git host. No malicious input is required; the secret leak is the impact.

findings.title: Rate-limit bypass for unlimited (response) message types when peer advertises RATE_LIMITS_V3, enabling unthrottled remote DoS
findings.vuln_class: remote DoS (missing rate limiting / unbounded processing)
findings.source: Untrusted P2P peer: capability list in the handshake (inbound_handshake.capabilities, parsed in perform_handshake) plus a flood of BINARY websocket frames whose Message.type is a v3 'response' protocol message type (e.g. respond_block, respond_blocks, respond_block_headers, respond_coin_state, respond_puzzle_state).
findings.sink: WSChiaConnection._read_one_message -> _route_incoming_message -> incoming_queue -> incoming_message_handler -> create_referenced_task(self._api_call(...)) -> metadata.method(self.api, full_message.data) (the full_node API handler for the response type, which performs block/coin-state validation work).
findings.data_flow: 1) A malicious full_node peer completes perform_handshake (ws_connection.py:253) and includes Capability.RATE_LIMITS_V3 in its capabilities; the local node, if it also has v3 active, sets both peer_capabilities and local_capabilities to include RATE_LIMITS_V3 (lines 313-336). 2) The peer then floods response-type messages. In _read_one_message (BINARY branch, lines 932-1018) the code enters the v3 block at line 965 because `Capability.RATE_LIMITS_V3 in self.peer_capabilities and ... in self.local_capabilities and message_type in rate_limits_v3`. 3) For response message types, rate_limits_v3[message_type].window_size is None (rate_limits_v3.py:36-70), so the inner `if window_size is not None ...` check (line 979) is skipped and NO disconnect/limit is applied. 4) Critically, because this `if` branch is taken, the `else` branch (lines 997-1000) that runs self.inbound_rate_limiter.process_msg_and_check is NOT executed, so the v2 per-type frequency/cumulative-size limiter is fully bypassed for these types. 5) limiter_msg stays None, the function returns full_message_loaded (line 1018), which is routed to incoming_queue and a new _api_call task is spawned per message (incoming_message_handler, lines 627-632) with no cap on concurrent api_tasks. The full-node handler for respond_block/respond_blocks is then invoked on attacker data and does real validation work. Each frame may be up to max_message_size = 50MB (server.py:52). The net effect: an attacker who simply advertises the v3 capability unlocks an unthrottled, unbounded ingestion+processing path for the heaviest (response) message types, which under the v2 limiter would have been frequency/size limited.
findings.preconditions: Attacker is a remote full_node peer that passes the cert-fingerprint TLS handshake (any cert chaining to the public Chia CA, which is shipped with all nodes) and advertises Capability.RATE_LIMITS_V3 so both sides activate v3. No special privilege beyond being able to open a peer connection.
findings.exploitability: Anonymous remote peer. The v3 response message types listed in rate_limits_v3 all have window_size=None and several (respond_block, respond_blocks) trigger expensive downstream processing. Bypassing the v2 inbound limiter for these types removes the per-minute count and cumulative-size caps that otherwise bound a peer's send rate, allowing sustained CPU/memory pressure and unbounded growth of spawned _api_call tasks.

result.reason: Code at keychain_server.py:154-172 matches the excerpt: kc_user/kc_service are taken verbatim from the request and used to construct/select a Keychain. However, this is not a security vulnerability. The user/service strings are documented (and function as) a data-store PARTITIONING mechanism, not an access-control boundary. The keychain RPC has no concept of per-client identity at all: authentication is solely the shared node TLS cert, so every authenticated client is equally privileged and already has full access to the default keychain (read all private keys, delete_all_keys, etc.). Letting a client name a different partition grants no capability the client did not already possess against the default partition, and there is no second, differently-privileged client whose partition could be 'stolen' or destroyed across a boundary. The finding's own data_flow narrative concedes 'partitioning provides no security boundary between clients sharing the node cert' — i.e., it is admitting there is no boundary to cross. delete_all_keys with an attacker-chosen partition is no more a 'destructive primitive' than delete_all_keys on the default keychain, which the same caller can already invoke. No privilege escalation, no isolation bypass, no traversal (Keychain backend keys are namespaced strings, not filesystem paths). The real (separate) risk surface is whether an unauthorized party can reach the websocket at all — that is the auth/origin question, not this partition-selection behavior. As scoped, this is behavior-as-designed at Info with no concrete exploit that crosses a trust boundary.
result.exploit_sketch: No meaningful exploit. A client authenticated to the daemon websocket could send {"command":"delete_all_keys","data":{"kc_user":"x","kc_service":"y"}} to target an arbitrary partition, but that same client can already call delete_all_keys/get_all_private_keys against the default keychain. No additional privilege, no other principal's data is protected by the partition, so no security boundary is crossed.

result.reason: Confirmed at websocket_base.py:150. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (WS_BASE_URL / WS_USER_BASE_URL), so the startswith("wss://") branch is taken on every default deployment, passing ssl=ssl.SSLContext(). A bare ssl.SSLContext() (no protocol arg) defaults to PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT in modern Python but the no-arg constructor leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, disabling both certificate and hostname validation. The sink is on the primary connection path (WSBase.open -> open_async), not dead code, not gated behind admin/superuser config, not disabled by default. The threat model is a network MITM (rogue Wi-Fi/router, ARP/DNS spoof, malicious proxy) on the path to the public Coinbase WS endpoint -- a genuine, attacker-controllable position requiring no privileges in the victim process. After connect, the authenticated JWT bearer (build_ws_jwt) is sent over this unverified channel during subscribe, so a MITM presenting a self-signed cert completes the handshake and captures the JWT plus account/market stream. Matches the documented known CWE-295 issue.
result.exploit_sketch: Attacker on the same LAN/Wi-Fi as the SDK consumer performs ARP or DNS spoofing for advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, terminates the WSS handshake with a self-signed cert. Because verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, the client accepts it. The attacker proxies (or drops) traffic, captures the JWT sent in the subscribe message, and can observe/inject account and market data frames.

result.reason: Verified at source. In _popen (base.py:476) text_cmd = " ".join(args) builds the full command string and is passed verbatim to cls.add_breadcrumb(text_cmd, ...) at base.py:519-525. For git, _clone (git.py:341-355) places the `source` URL directly into argv, so when an admin sets component.repo/push to a credentialed URL (https://user:token@host/repo.git — a Weblate-supported pattern), the embedded credentials appear in text_cmd. sanitize_error_message (base.py:547, the no-op default) is applied only to process output on a nonzero return code (base.py:527), never to text_cmd. add_breadcrumb forwards message+data to sentry_sdk.add_breadcrumb (errors.py:165-173), so the credential-bearing command string is recorded as a Sentry breadcrumb and attached to subsequent error events. This is a genuine CWE-532 credential-exposure-to-logging issue. It is gated by SENTRY_DSN being configured (no exposure otherwise), and the credentials are admin's own and only reach the deployment's own Sentry, which is why Low is appropriate — there is no cross-tenant or privilege-escalation impact.
result.exploit_sketch: A project admin (lower-priv than superuser) configures a component with repo = https://gituser:ghp_SECRETTOKEN@github.com/org/repo.git. On clone, _popen runs ['git', <auth args>, 'clone', ..., '--', 'https://gituser:ghp_SECRETTOKEN@github.com/org/repo.git', target]; text_cmd joins these including the credentialed URL and is sent to add_breadcrumb. When any later VCS operation in that request/transaction errors and report_error fires to Sentry (SENTRY_DSN set), the breadcrumb trail — including the plaintext token — is uploaded to the Sentry project, visible to anyone with Sentry access (potentially broader than those entitled to the repo credential).

An active MITM (malicious Wi-Fi/AP, ARP/DNS spoof, rogue proxy) on the path to advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com presents any self-signed certificate. Because websockets.connect is invoked with ssl=ssl.SSLContext() (a bare context whose CPython defaults are verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False), the TLS handshake completes without validating the cert chain or hostname. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, so the vulnerable branch is the standard path. After connect, _build_subscription_message() (line ~561) calls jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(api_key, api_secret) and subscribe_async() sends the authenticated JWT bearer token over the unverified channel (self.websocket.send). The attacker captures the JWT and can observe/inject account and market stream data.
result.reason: Verified at websocket_base.py:150: ssl=ssl.SSLContext() if self.base_url.startswith("wss://") else None. A bare ssl.SSLContext() in CPython leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False — no certificate or hostname validation. The correct call would be ssl.create_default_context(). No mitigation exists between source and sink: there is no scheme allow-list that affects TLS, no cert pinning, no verify override, no load_default_certs, no SafeLoader-style defense relevant here. The default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com, so the vulnerable branch executes by default, and authenticated JWTs are subsequently transmitted over the unverified channel. The finding survives the mitigation lens. This is a known/confirmed issue (CWE-295), not a novel re-report; CVSS 7.5 (High) is consistent with the SDK's anchor vector.

Code matches the excerpt: keychain_server.py:317-326 returns pk + entropy.hex() for all keys, reachable via server.py:436-437 (command in keychain_commands -> KeychainServer.handle_command). However, this is by-design functionality gated by a real trust boundary, not a vulnerability. server.py:199 builds the listener SSL context via ssl_context_for_server, which at chia/server/server.py:71 sets verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED against the node's private CA. Thus only a client presenting a certificate signed by the local Chia private CA can even connect. That is precisely the intended trust model: the wallet GUI/CLI fetch private keys this way for signing and backups. An attacker capable of presenting a CA-signed client cert already possesses the private CA key, which lives on the same host as the keyring itself, so no privilege/trust boundary is crossed - obtaining the prerequisite cert is equivalent to already having local filesystem access to the secret material. The candidate's own data_flow concedes the only gates are mutual TLS and an unlocked keyring; there is no separate per-client authorization being bypassed. No working exploit exists for any party who is not already a fully trusted local component. The claimed 'Low' severity itself reflects that this is not a genuine boundary violation.
result.exploit_sketch: No realistic exploit. Triggering get_all_private_keys requires a TLS client cert signed by the node's private CA (chia/server/server.py:71 verify_mode=CERT_REQUIRED). Acquiring that cert/key requires local access to the same files/keyring the keys live in, so there is no privilege escalation or remote/unauthorized path.

result.reason: Confirmed in source. coinbase/websocket/websocket_base.py:150 passes ssl=ssl.SSLContext() to websockets.connect whenever base_url starts with wss://, which is the default (constants.py: WS_BASE_URL = "wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com", used as the default base_url in __init__). A default ssl.SSLContext() leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so neither the server certificate chain nor the hostname is validated. The authenticated JWT bearer (jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt over api_key/api_secret) is then sent over this unverified channel during subscribe. This is a genuine CWE-295 instance and matches the project's already-documented confirmed finding. The only realistic precondition is an active MITM network position, which is exactly the standard threat model for this weakness and is reflected in the CVSS AC:H component.
result.exploit_sketch: 1) Attacker gains active on-path position between the SDK consumer and advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com (rogue AP, ARP/DNS spoofing, or compromised proxy). 2) Application uses the default WSBase/WSClient (base_url defaults to wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com), calls open() then subscribe(). 3) open_async() establishes the WSS connection with ssl=ssl.SSLContext(); because verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, the attacker presents any self-signed certificate for the hostname and the handshake completes without error. 4) The client sends the subscription message containing the JWT built by jwt_generator.build_ws_jwt(api_key, api_secret). 5) Attacker captures the JWT (valid ~120s) and can replay it to authenticate to Coinbase, and can read/inject account and market stream frames in both directions. Impact: theft of a valid auth token plus full read/tamper of the live data stream.

result.reason:
The finding is refuted on two independent grounds.

(1) No regression vs v2: The candidate's central claim is that v3 bypasses the v2 limiter, which "would have frequency/size limited" response types. That is false. In rate_limits.py:111-118, the v2 limiter treats response message types as `Unlimited` too — only a max_size check applies, never a frequency/cumulative limit. The inline comment confirms this is intentional: "this message type is not rate limited. This is used for response messages and must be combined with banning peers sending unsolicited responses of this type." v3 (rate_limits_v3.py:36-70, window_size=None for responses) is behaviorally equivalent to v2 for these types. Advertising RATE_LIMITS_V3 unlocks nothing new.

(2) The cited sink does not perform validation work — it disconnects. Solicited responses are consumed in _route_incoming_message (ws_connection.py:634-648): a response whose message.id matches a pending_request is delivered to the waiting caller and never enters incoming_queue/_api_call. An UNSOLICITED response (no matching id) does reach _api_call, but there are no registered full_node API handler methods named respond_block/respond_blocks/respond_coin_state/respond_puzzle_state etc. (grep confirms none exist). At ws_connection.py:498-503, metadata is None, so the code raises ProtocolError(INVALID_PROTOCOL_MESSAGE); the exception path (606-617) closes and bans the peer. The attacker is disconnected on the FIRST unsolicited response, so there is no unbounded ingestion and no block/coin-state validation performed on attacker data. The shared defense (ban unsolicited responses) is present and effective in both v2 and v3 paths.
result.exploit_sketch: N/A — an unsolicited response message of a v3 'response' type is not dispatched to any API handler; the first such message causes metadata is None at ws_connection.py:501 and the peer is banned/disconnected. A solicited response is consumed by request/response correlation and never reaches the API. No unthrottled processing path exists.

findings.title: SSRF: data_layer RPC subscribe/add_mirror accept arbitrary URLs that the node later fetches
findings.source: DataLayerRpcApi.subscribe / add_mirror request body field `urls` (chia/data_layer/data_layer_rpc_api.py:360-398,468-474), reachable over the data_layer RPC websocket/HTTP endpoint
findings.sink: aiohttp ClientSession.get(server_info.url + "/" + filename, proxy=proxy_url) in http_download (chia/data_layer/download_data.py:313) and session.post(d.url + "/handle_download") in DataLayer.get_downloader (chia/data_layer/data_layer.py:717)
findings.data_flow: RPC handler DataLayerRpcApi.subscribe(request) -> request.get('urls') -> DataLayer.subscribe(store_id, urls) (data_layer.py:869) only does url.rstrip('/') with no scheme/host/IP validation -> ServerInfo(url) persisted via data_store.subscribe -> background DataLayer.fetch_data loop reads get_available_servers_for_store (data_layer.py:624-628) -> insert_from_delta_file(server_info=...) -> download_file -> http_download performs GET on server_info.url + '/' + filename (download_data.py:313). add_mirror feeds the same path and additionally publishes the attacker URL on-chain so other subscribing nodes fetch it (update_subscriptions_from_wallet -> get_available_servers_for_store).
findings.preconditions: Caller can reach the data_layer RPC port. Auth boundary is mutual TLS using the node's private CA (ssl_context_for_server, CERT_REQUIRED); a client cert minted from the locally-readable CA, or an exposed RPC port plus CA leak, satisfies it. For the on-chain mirror variant, any actor who can place a mirror coin on chain can influence victim nodes' fetch targets with no RPC access.
findings.exploitability: No allow-list, no scheme restriction, no internal-IP/metadata-endpoint blocking, no DNS-rebinding guard. An attacker can point the node at internal services (e.g. 169.254.169.254, localhost admin ports) and observe via error/timing behavior, or use it as a blind request forger. Impact limited to GET/POST with fixed paths and a size cap, hence Medium.
findings.code_excerpt:
parsed_urls = [url.rstrip("/") for url in urls]
subscription = Subscription(store_id, [ServerInfo(url, 0, 0) for url in parsed_urls])

findings.title: Full Python traceback returned to RPC clients on handler exceptions
findings.source: Any exception raised inside any RPC endpoint handler invoked via wrap_http_handler.inner (request body drives which handler and with what params)
findings.sink: res_object['traceback'] = traceback.format_exc() returned in the JSON HTTP response (chia/rpc/util.py:90-97 via obj_to_response)
findings.data_flow: WebServer routes POST -> wrap_http_handler.inner(request) (util.py:78) -> f(request_data); on Exception the except block builds res_object including the full formatted traceback (util.py:87-95) and returns it to the client via obj_to_response. The traceback exposes absolute filesystem paths, module/function names, and internal state.
findings.preconditions: Caller can reach the RPC endpoint (same mutual-TLS boundary as above). Triggerable by sending malformed parameters to virtually any endpoint.
findings.exploitability: Trivially triggered; leaks internal paths and code structure. Defense-in-depth severity since the channel is already authenticated, but it is an unconditional verbatim stack-trace return, unlike the websocket path which only logs.

result.reason: The finding's core premise — that response message types would have been frequency/size limited under the v2 limiter and that v3 introduces a bypass — is false. In rate_limit_numbers.py the heaviest response types are already declared Unlimited(...) in v2 (respond_blocks: Unlimited(50MB), respond_block: Unlimited(2MB), etc.). In rate_limits.py:111-118 the v2 process_msg_and_check explicitly applies ONLY a per-message size cap to Unlimited types and returns None with no frequency or cumulative-count limiting; the in-code comment states these "must be combined with banning peers sending unsolicited responses." v3 preserves identical semantics (window_size=None == unlimited). So nothing is bypassed — there was no v2 frequency limit on these types to begin with. The real defense, present and unchanged in both schemes, is application-layer: (1) _route_incoming_message (ws_connection.py:634) routes solicited responses straight to pending_requests, never to the api queue; (2) for unsolicited responses, the full_node handlers do NOT perform validation work — respond_blocks (full_node_api.py:502-508) and respond_block (511-518) immediately log 'unsolicited' and call peer.close(RATE_LIMITER_BAN_SECONDS), i.e. the first such frame bans the peer. The claimed 'flood of heavy validation work with no cap' is therefore impossible: the sink does the opposite of what the finding asserts. The 50MB max_message_size cap and the size cap in the Unlimited path both still apply per-frame. No regression, no reachable unbounded-processing sink, mitigation present.
result.exploit_sketch: No working exploit. A peer that advertises RATE_LIMITS_V3 and floods respond_block/respond_blocks frames gets banned on the very first unsolicited frame (full_node_api.py:508/517), so the asserted unthrottled processing path never materializes. Identical behavior existed under v2 (these types were Unlimited there too), so there is no bypass to exploit.

result.reason: The finding's central premise is false. It claims the v3 path bypasses a v2 frequency/cumulative-size limit for response message types. But in the v2 limiter (rate_limit_numbers.py), the cited heaviest response types are ALSO classified as Unlimited: respond_blocks = Unlimited(50MB), respond_block = Unlimited(2MB), reject_block/reject_blocks = Unlimited(100). rate_limits.py lines 111-118 show Unlimited types receive NO frequency limit and NO cumulative-size limit under v2 either - only a per-message size cap. The explicit code comment (lines 112-114) states Unlimited is used for response messages and 'must be combined with banning peers sending unsolicited responses of this type.' So v3 setting window_size=None for these types preserves the exact same posture v2 had; it does not remove a protection. The DoS guard for unsolicited responses in BOTH schemes is (a) the per-message transport size cap (max_message_size, enforced by aiohttp before this code, and the WSCloseCode.MESSAGE_TOO_BIG path at line 1021), and (b) full_node API handler rejection/banning of unsolicited responses. The v3 if/else is a deliberate replacement of v2 for supported types, not an accidental bypass. The few response types that v2 aggregate-limits (respond_coin_state, respond_puzzle_state = RLSettings) require an outstanding local subscription/request and the handlers drop unsolicited ones, and these are not the 'heaviest' types the finding leads with. The claim 'which under the v2 limiter would have been frequency/size limited' is incorrect for the headline message types. Source and sink are real and attacker-reachable, but the claimed regression/bypass does not exist, so the High-severity DoS as described is refuted.
result.exploit_sketch: N/A - no privilege/protection differential between v2 and v3 for the cited types; flooding Unlimited response types is equally (un)constrained under both, and is bounded by the per-message size cap plus handler-level unsolicited-response rejection. Any residual flood concern (raw bytes ingestion) is pre-existing and identical regardless of the v3 capability, not introduced by it.

result.reason: The traceback is indeed returned (util.py:87-95), but the RPC HTTP server is started with mutual-TLS: RpcServer.create builds ssl_context via ssl_context_for_server using the node's PRIVATE CA cert/key (rpc_server.py:177-206), which requires the connecting client to present a certificate signed by that private CA. Any party able to complete the handshake and reach wrap_http_handler.inner is therefore already a fully-trusted client holding the private client cert -- the same trust level that grants stop_node, open_connection, and wallet/key endpoints. To such a client, a traceback exposing local filesystem paths and module names is not a meaningful secret; it discloses nothing beyond what a fully-authorized operator already controls. An attacker merely reaching the TCP port (even bound beyond localhost) cannot complete mTLS and never invokes the handler, so no traceback is returned. The exploit therefore requires the unrealistic precondition of already possessing the trusted client certificate, and even then yields no privilege escalation or secret disclosure. This is accepted intentional debug behavior, not a real vulnerability under the stated threat model.
result.exploit_sketch: Would require: obtain the node's private-CA-signed client certificate, then POST malformed JSON to an RPC endpoint to provoke an exception and read res_object['traceback']. But holding that cert already grants full RPC control, so no escalation results.

result.reason: The data flow described is accurate at the code level: DataLayerRpcApi.subscribe/add_mirror accept a `urls` list, DataLayer.subscribe (data_layer.py:870) only does url.rstrip('/') with no scheme/host/IP validation, and http_download (download_data.py:313) performs session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename) on the unvalidated URL. So the sink is reachable and there is no URL allowlist. However the finding is refuted on two grounds. (1) No privilege boundary is crossed for the standard SSRF threat: the data_layer RPC server is started with ssl_context_for_server using the node's PRIVATE CA (rpc_server.py:182,199-205) and bound to self_hostname, so it is mutual-TLS authenticated. Only the node operator (or a client holding the operator-issued cert) can call subscribe. An operator instructing their OWN node to fetch a URL of their choosing is the documented, intended function of the data layer (it is a content-distribution layer whose whole purpose is to subscribe to and download delta files from arbitrary mirror servers). That is not SSRF in any meaningful sense — there is no confused-deputy: the requester and the fetcher trust boundary are the same party. (2) The candidate provides no auth/origin bypass that would let an unauthenticated network attacker reach the endpoint; the threat model's premise ('a client able to reach the RPC port') is gated by mTLS with a private CA and is not satisfied by the cited code. The add_mirror 'on-chain publication so other nodes fetch it' angle is likewise the protocol's intended mirror-announcement behavior; other nodes voluntarily subscribed and fetching mirror URLs is core functionality, fetched data is hash-validated against the singleton root, and there is no internal-network pivot beyond a generic outbound HTTP GET. The exploit therefore requires the unrealistic precondition of already holding RPC credentials, at which point the 'attacker' is the operator.
result.exploit_sketch: To 'trigger' it one must already possess the node's RPC client certificate (operator-only), then POST {"id": "<store_hex>", "urls": ["http://169.254.169.254"]} to /subscribe. The node would then GET http://169.254.169.254/<filename>. But this is the operator commanding their own node — no privilege boundary crossed, response is not reflected to any other party, and content is hash-verified, so no usable cloud-metadata exfiltration or confused-deputy impact. No working cross-trust-boundary exploit exists from the actual code.

result.executive_summary: Three verified findings span two targets. The clear lead is the Coinbase Advanced Python SDK bare ssl.SSLContext() certificate-validation bypass (CWE-295, High, CVSS 7.5), already catalogued internally as PY-SSL-001 but NOT YET SUBMITTED. It is the only High-severity, high-payout item: Coinbase runs a top-tier paying HackerOne program ($200-$1,000,000 tiers) that explicitly covers open-source projects, and the finding leaks an authenticated JWT bearer to an on-path MITM. The two Weblate findings are genuine but Low-severity CWE-532 credential-in-logs/breadcrumbs issues whose impact requires existing access to logs/Sentry; Weblate's program pays modestly. Recommendation: prioritize and submit the Coinbase SSL issue now with a working MITM PoC; submit the two Weblate log-leak issues together as a single hardening report at Low.

ranked_findings.title: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
ranked_findings.exploitability: Active on-path attacker (rogue Wi-Fi/AP, ARP/DNS spoof, malicious proxy). No privilege within the victim process. Default base_url is wss://advanced-trade-ws.coinbase.com so the vulnerable branch executes on every default deployment. Bare ssl.SSLContext() leaves verify_mode=CERT_NONE and check_hostname=False, so a self-signed cert is accepted and the authenticated JWT bearer (build_ws_jwt) is captured.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Only High-severity item and the only one against a top-paying program. Confirmed by a 3/3 verification panel and re-confirmed in source (ssl.SSLContext() at line 150 on the primary open_async path, not gated/dead). Leaks a live authenticated JWT plus full read/inject of account and market-data frames against a crypto-exchange SDK - exactly the monetary-impact category Coinbase pays well for. ALREADY-TRACKED internally as PY-SSL-001; not yet submitted, so still actionable. Coinbase scope explicitly includes open-source projects.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Build the MITM PoC in assets/poc/ssl_mitm/: stand up a mitmproxy/self-signed-cert endpoint, point a default WSClient at it, and capture the subscribe-message JWT to prove acceptance of an invalid cert and token disclosure. Confirm the SDK is in-scope on hackerone.com/coinbase, then submit with the PoC, the one-line fix (ssl.create_default_context() or omit ssl=), and docs/findings/INSECURE_SSL_websocket_base.md. Mark report as PY-SSL-001 in the dedup store on submission.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Coinbase HackerOne: minimum-per-tier payouts ~ $200 to $1,000,000, internal monetary-impact risk rating, program covers all Coinbase software including open-source projects. A High CWE-295 in an official SDK leaking auth tokens is a credible mid-tier monetary payout; argue real-world impact (token replay, market-data tampering) to push the tier upward. Internal status: PY-SSL-001, already-tracked, not yet submitted.

ranked_findings.title: Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
ranked_findings.exploitability: No malicious input needed; secret leak is the impact. Requires read access to weblate.vcs logs or Sentry breadcrumbs. Org-scoped Git-provider token from *_CREDENTIALS settings is embedded as username:token@host by authenticate_url() and logged verbatim at INFO (base log() does not sanitize; sanitize_error_message is a no-op applied only to error paths). Triggers whenever a merge-request backend configures its fork remote over an http(s) push scheme.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Cleanest of the two Weblate issues: deterministic INFO-level emission on a normal push path (not gated on SENTRY_DSN), so exposure occurs in any deployment shipping logs to aggregation. Defensible CWE-532 with a clear redaction fix and higher finder confidence (0.7) than the breadcrumb variant.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Confirm Weblate's HackerOne scope accepts logging-hygiene/CWE-532 reports and current reward posture. Write a minimal repro: configure a component with GITHUB_CREDENTIALS http(s) push and capture the INFO log line showing https://user:TOKEN@host. Propose redacting credentials in self.log()/authenticate_url before logging. Bundle with finding rank 3 into one report.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Weblate runs a HackerOne program but is a small open-source project historically associated with low/modest monetary rewards or swag; Low log-hygiene issues typically land at the bottom tier. Value is largely reputation/CVE credit. Program existence confirmed via search; specific reward tiers not published - reasoning partly from prior knowledge, flagged as such.

ranked_findings.title: Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args
ranked_findings.exploitability: Project admin sets component.repo/push to a credentialed URL; _popen joins argv into text_cmd including the full URL and passes it to add_breadcrumb. Exposure only materializes if SENTRY_DSN is configured AND a later VCS op in the same transaction errors and fires report_error. Credentials are the admin's own and reach only the deployment's own Sentry - no cross-tenant or privilege-escalation impact.
ranked_findings.why_high_value: Real CWE-532 and a useful companion to rank 2, but lowest priority: doubly conditional (SENTRY_DSN set + a subsequent error), lowest finder confidence (0.5), and self-owned credentials reaching the operator's own Sentry. Best value is as a second data point in a single 'redact credentials before logging/breadcrumbs' hardening report.
ranked_findings.suggested_next_step: Submit jointly with rank 2 as one consolidated credential-redaction report. Optionally add a scanner rule (CWE-532: credential-bearing URL flowing into a logging/breadcrumb sink) with paired vuln_*/safe_* fixtures under tests/fixtures/ to catch both sites and regressions. Do not open a separate disclosure for this alone.
ranked_findings.program_payout_note: Same Weblate program as rank 2; expect bottom-tier/swag or merge-as-hardening rather than a meaningful bounty. Consolidating with rank 2 avoids duplicate-triage friction and presents a stronger single narrative.

result.program_scope_notes:
Coinbase (HackerOne, hackerone.com/coinbase): Active, well-funded paying program. Public tiers list minimum-per-tier payouts spanning roughly $200 to $1,000,000 (page last updated Mar 2026), with final amounts set by an internal monetary-impact risk rating that can exceed listed minimums. Scope explicitly covers all Coinbase software including open-source projects, so coinbase-advanced-py is plausibly in scope - VERIFY the exact asset listing before submission. Highest payout potential of the three. Coinbase separately runs a $5M onchain-focused program on Cantina (not relevant to this SDK finding).

Weblate (HackerOne): A program exists (referenced on BugBountyHunter.com and HackerOne). Weblate is a small open-source/libre project; published reward tiers were not retrievable in search, and prior knowledge suggests rewards are modest-to-low (often swag, small monetary amounts, or CVE/hall-of-fame credit) - flagged as reasoned from prior knowledge, not freshly confirmed. Both Weblate findings are Low CWE-532 log/breadcrumb credential-exposure issues requiring existing log/Sentry access; payout expectations should be low. CONFIRM current scope and CWE-532 eligibility before submitting.

Chia Network (HackerOne, hackerone.com/chia_network): Confirmed active paying program (migrated from Bugcrowd to HackerOne Feb 2024). However, NONE of the three findings target Chia, so it is out of scope for this batch and carries no payout relevance here.

2026 context: HackerOne reduced some bounty rewards (The Register, May 2026); calibrate expectations slightly downward. Tooling note: WebSearch was available; exact private program reward tables on HackerOne policy pages were not directly retrievable, so specific Weblate/Chia tier figures rely partly on prior knowledge as noted.

Sources: https://hackerone.com/coinbase ; https://www.coinbase.com/blog/updating-the-coinbase-bug-bounty-program ; https://hackerone.com/chia_network ; https://www.chia.net/2024/02/29/bug-bounty-program-moving-to-hackerone-for-enhanced-security/ ; https://www.bugbountyhunter.com/program?id=weblate ; https://www.theregister.com/security/2026/05/21/hackerone-takes-an-axe-to-its-bug-bounty-rewards/5244458
recommended_priority_order: WebSocket TLS certificate verification disabled via bare ssl.SSLContext()
Authenticated push URL containing credentials written to logs at INFO level
Repository URL with embedded credentials logged to error-tracking breadcrumbs in command args
